Table rules: Do not use the non‑existent Table.IndexOfSelf property; instead obtain the table index from the NodeCollection that holds the tables.
When iterating a NodeCollection, cast each node to Table before accessing Table members.
Prefer a for‑loop (or tables.IndexOf(table)) to get a reliable sequential number for each table.
Add the required using directives: Aspose.Words and Aspose.Words.Tables.
Do not assign to read‑only properties such as Cell.FirstParagraph; instead add content via the cell's Paragraphs collection.
Always ensure a new row has at least one cell before inserting content (use Row.EnsureMinimum()).
When adding a paragraph to a cell, use cell.Paragraphs.Add(paragraph) or cell.AppendChild(paragraph).
Use fully qualified types (Document, Table, Row, Cell, Paragraph, Run) from the Aspose.Words namespace.
Do not use a non‑existent Table.Split method; instead clone the table without rows and move the required rows manually.
Clone the original table with Clone(false) to preserve table properties (borders, width, etc.) while creating an empty table.
Iterate backwards when removing rows from the original table to avoid index shifting.
Insert the newly created table after the original using ParentNode.InsertAfter.
Cast the parent node of the table to CompositeNode before calling InsertAfter.
Keep the original logic of collecting rows before removal to avoid index shifting issues.
When moving rows, remove them from the original table and prepend them to the new table to keep the original order.
Use a shallow clone of the source table (originalTable.Clone(false)) to copy its formatting instead of the non‑existent CloneStyleFrom method.
Do not attempt to call CloneStyleFrom on Table; it is not part of the Aspose.Words API.
When cloning a Table, cast the result back to Table.
Keep the row‑moving logic unchanged; rows are moved from the original to the cloned table.
Ensure the DocumentBuilder cursor is positioned after the original table before inserting a page break and the new table.
Range rules: Validate the split index to avoid out‑of‑range errors.
Saving/Export rules: Keep the document save call if the surrounding framework expects a write operation, even when no changes are made.
Layout rules: Use CompositeNode (or a derived type such as Body, Section, Paragraph) when calling InsertAfter/InsertBefore because these methods are defined on CompositeNode, not on the base Node class.
